summary: The main claim is: I’m pretty sure that’s not how ChatGPT generates stories.
Twenty stories This introduction is about the 20 stories I had ChatGPT generate this morning (10.13.23).

The main claim is: If a part of the network has the same activations for both mazes, it is not related to the distinction between "go up to get the cheese" and "go right to get the cheese".
On the one hand, zeroing these 16 channels changes a lot, but on the other hand - the mouse would still go to the cheese and not to the top right.

The main claim is: In this model, humans do not have cleanly separated values, world models, and decision theory!
Seen from a stereotypical view of human behavior, values and preferences about the world are decoupled from our world model and beliefs.
The main claim is: Understand gradient descent well enough to employ this strategy in theory, as well as recognize when the model is in training.
First, we lay out a scenario where gradient filtering could play a role, and how this benefits the model.

The main claim is: In this paper authors explore the scaling properties of mixed-modal generative models, discovering new scaling laws that unify the contributions of individual modalities and the interactions between them.

I find most interesting that they have found so-called competition barrier - when training with multiple modalities, after a certain number of parameters/data, the loss is smaller than if the modalities were trained independently.

The main claim is: There should be commitments that if model capabilities reach a certain level, labs will not scale up or deploy models until they have reached a certain level of alignment.
We're a new team at ARC building capability evaluations (and in the future, alignment evaluations) for advanced ML models.
The main claim is: So: the broadness of the basin is determined by the number and size of independent features in the network .
The Hessian of the network at the optimum contains the L2 norm of the products of all the features in the network with each other.

The main claim is: This is the introduction to a sequence of posts that describe our models for Pragmatic AI Safety.
We should instead let the broader ML community take care of general capabilities, and work on safety problems that are viable with current capabilities.
